nodes.standard_name: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
nodes.original_text_name: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
nodes.context: Palestinian Marxist-Leninist organization founded in 1967, second-largest PLO faction.
Umbrella organization of Palestinian national movement.
Largest faction within the PLO, more moderate than PFLP.
Military wing of the PFLP.
Palestinian Islamist group, allied with PFLP in Gaza war.
Predecessor organization to the PFLP, founded in 1953.
Internal military organization of ANM established in late 1963.
Renamed PAC, a military unit of ANM.
Military wing of the PLO, collaborated with ANM.
Commando group established by ANM in collaboration with PLA.
Syria-based group led by Ahmed Jibril, merged into PFLP.

nodes.standard_name: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine – General Command
nodes.original_text_name: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine – General Command
nodes.context: Breakaway faction from PFLP led by Ahmed Jibril in 1968.
Splinter from PFLP-GC led by Ahmed Zarur.

nodes.standard_name: Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine
nodes.original_text_name: Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine
nodes.context: Maoist organization that split from PFLP in 1969.

nodes.standard_name: Popular Revolutionary Front for the Liberation of Palestine
nodes.original_text_name: Popular Revolutionary Front for the Liberation of Palestine
nodes.context: Splinter from PFLP formed in 1972.
Damascus-based coalition opposed to Oslo Accords, PFLP was a founding member.
Lebanese Islamist group, allied with PFLP post-Oslo.
Leftist group allied with PFLP post-Oslo.
Gaza-based group allied with PFLP.
Separate political party formed from PFLP's Jordan branch in 1990.
Colombian left-wing group, PFLP developed ties with.
Japanese left-wing group, PFLP developed ties with.
Islamist group, marginalized PFLP's role.
Broke away from PFLP in 1967.

nodes.standard_name: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
nodes.original_text_name: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
nodes.context: Palestinian Marxist-Leninist organization founded in 1967, second-largest group in the PLO.
Largest group in the Palestine Liberation Organization, more moderate than PFLP.
Umbrella organization of Palestinian groups, includes PFLP and Fatah.
Military wing of the PFLP.
Militant group that collaborated with PFLP's Waddie Haddad in the Lod Airport massacre.
Militia that clashed with PFLP in South Lebanon in 1990.
Palestinian militant group that fought alongside PFLP's Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades in the Gaza Strip during the 2023-2024 war.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: It has consistently been the second-largest of the groups forming the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO), the largest being Fatah.
edges.context: PFLP is a member group of the PLO.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: A secular organization, the PFLP has generally taken a hard line on Palestinian national aspirations, opposing the more moderate stance of Fatah.
edges.context: PFLP opposes Fatah's moderate stance.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: The military wing of the PFLP is called the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades.
edges.context: Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades is the paramilitary wing of PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: More recently, the group has participated in the ongoing Gaza war alongside Hamas and other allied Palestinian factions.
edges.context: PFLP fights alongside Hamas in the Gaza war.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: The PFLP grew out of the Harakat al-Qawmiyyin al-Arab, or Arab Nationalist Movement (ANM), founded in 1953 by George Habash.
edges.context: PFLP originated from the ANM.

edges.source: Arab Nationalist Movement
edges.evidence_quote: In late 1963 the ANM leadership established the Palestinian Action Command (PAC), an internal military organisation with nominal authority over the ANM's Palestinian members.
edges.context: PAC was established by ANM as its military wing.

edges.source: Palestinian Action Command
edges.evidence_quote: the PAC's name was changed to Revenge Youth Organisation (RYO).
edges.context: PAC was renamed to RYO.

edges.source: Arab Nationalist Movement
edges.evidence_quote: In collaboration with the Palestine Liberation Organisation's (PLO) Palestinian Liberation Army (PLA), the ANM established the Abtal al-Audah (Heroes of Return) commando group in early summer 1966.
edges.context: ANM collaborated with PLA to establish Heroes of Return.

edges.source: Arab Nationalist Movement
edges.evidence_quote: the ANM established the Abtal al-Audah (Heroes of Return) commando group in early summer 1966. The ANM exercised de facto control, unbeknownst to the PLO, which funded the outfit.
edges.context: ANM controlled Heroes of Return.

edges.source: Palestinian Liberation Army
edges.evidence_quote: In collaboration with the Palestine Liberation Organisation's (PLO) Palestinian Liberation Army (PLA), the ANM established the Abtal al-Audah (Heroes of Return) commando group in early summer 1966.
edges.context: PLA collaborated with ANM to establish Heroes of Return.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: Included within the discussions, as well as the ANM (including the RYO), were Heroes of Return (officially a collaboration with the PLO but actually under ANM control), Ahmed Jibril's Syria-based Palestinian Liberation Front, and a group of Jordanian Nasserists led by the former army officer Ahmed Za'rur. These groups combined to form the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine (PFLP) which was announced to the public on 11 December 1967.
edges.context: Palestinian Liberation Front merged into PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.target: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine – General Command
edges.evidence_quote: In October 1968, Ahmed Jibril led a break away from the PFLP to form the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine – General Command (PFLP-GC), and was joined by Ahmed Zarur and his followers.
edges.context: PFLP-GC split from PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine – General Command
edges.evidence_quote: Zarur later split from the PFLP-GC to form the Arab Palestine Organisation.
edges.context: Arab Palestine Organisation split from PFLP-GC.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.target: Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: In 1969, the Democratic Front for the Liberation of Palestine (DFLP) formed as a separate, ostensibly Maoist, organization under Nayef Hawatmeh and Yasser Abd Rabbo, initially as the PDFLP.
edges.context: DFLP split from PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.target: Popular Revolutionary Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: In 1972, the Popular Revolutionary Front for the Liberation of Palestine was formed following a split in PFLP.
edges.context: Popular Revolutionary Front split from PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: In December 1993 PFLP withdrew from the PLO and became one of the ten founding members of the Damascus-based Alliance of Palestinian Forces.
edges.context: PFLP was a founding member of APF.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: The PFLP developed contacts at this time with Islamic fundamentalist groups linked to Iran – both Palestinian Hamas, and the Lebanon-based Hezbollah.
edges.context: PFLP developed contacts with Hezbollah.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: The PFLP has therefore formed alliances with other leftist groups formed within the Palestinian Authority, including the Palestinian People's Party and the Popular Resistance Committees of Gaza.
edges.context: PFLP formed an alliance with Palestinian People's Party.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: The PFLP has therefore formed alliances with other leftist groups formed within the Palestinian Authority, including the Palestinian People's Party and the Popular Resistance Committees of Gaza.
edges.context: PFLP formed an alliance with Popular Resistance Committees.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: In 1990, the PFLP transformed its Jordan branch into a separate political party, the Jordanian Popular Democratic Unity Party.
edges.context: PFLP's Jordan branch became the Jordanian Popular Democratic Unity Party.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: From its foundation, the PFLP sought superpower patrons, early on developing ties with the Soviet Union, the People's Republic of China, and, at various times, with regional powers such as Syria, South Yemen, Libya, North Korea, and Iraq, as well as with left-wing groups around the world, including the FARC and the Japanese Red Army.
edges.context: PFLP developed ties with FARC.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: From its foundation, the PFLP sought superpower patrons, early on developing ties with the Soviet Union, the People's Republic of China, and, at various times, with regional powers such as Syria, South Yemen, Libya, North Korea, and Iraq, as well as with left-wing groups around the world, including the FARC and the Japanese Red Army.
edges.context: PFLP developed ties with Japanese Red Army.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: The dissolution of the Soviet Union together with the rise of Islamism—and particularly the increased popularity of the Islamist groups Hamas and Palestinian Islamic Jihad – disoriented many left activists who had looked towards the Soviet Union, and has marginalized the PFLP's role in Palestinian politics and armed resistance.
edges.context: Palestinian Islamic Jihad marginalized PFLP's role.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: In 1967, Palestinian Popular Struggle Front (PPSF) broke away from the PFLP.
edges.context: PPSF split from PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: It has consistently been the second-largest of the groups forming the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO), the largest being Fatah.
edges.context: PFLP and Fatah are both members of the PLO, with PFLP being the second-largest and Fatah the largest.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: It has consistently been the second-largest of the groups forming the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO)
edges.context: PFLP is a member group of the PLO.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: The military wing of the PFLP is called the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades.
edges.context: Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades is the military wing of the PFLP.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: On 30 May 1972, 28 passengers were gunned down at Ben Gurion International Airport by members of the Japanese Red Army in collaboration with the PFLP's Waddie Haddad
edges.context: Japanese Red Army collaborated with a PFLP faction in the Lod Airport massacre.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: On 8 June 1990 a clash occurred near Shuwayya in South Lebanon in which four members of the PFLP were killed by the South Lebanon Army (SLA).
edges.context: PFLP and SLA clashed in South Lebanon, resulting in PFLP casualties.

edges.source: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine
edges.evidence_quote: the Abu Ali Mustafa Brigades published videos of it storming Israeli watchtowers during the October 7 attacks, and has since fought alongside Hamas and other allied factions in multiple battles inside the Gaza Strip.
edges.context: PFLP's military wing fought alongside Hamas during the Gaza war (2023-present).
